subcategory: East African and Malagasy ornamental cycad

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–12; Malagasy Cycad is a large, palm-like cycad native to Madagascar, the Comoros Islands, East African coastal forests (Mozambique, Tanzania, Kenya), and the Mascarene Islands — producing an arching crown of long, glossy, bright-green fronds on a stout trunk, and growing to impressive size over many decades in warm, frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Cycas thouarsii is drought tolerant once established and does not tolerate waterlogged soils — excellent drainage is essential; water deeply but infrequently and apply balanced slow-release fertilizer once or twice per year. Cycas thouarsii (Malagasy Cycad, East African Cycad, or Madagascar Cycad) is a large, striking cycad native to the coastal and lowland forests of Madagascar, the Comoros, and the East African coastline (Mozambique, Tanzania, coastal Kenya), making it the only Cycas species native to Africa or its offshore islands. The plant is a slow-growing, long-lived, architectural specimen cycad developing a stout, palm-like trunk topped by a crown of long (up to 6 feet), graceful, arching, pinnately compound fronds with glossy, bright-green, narrowly lanceolate leaflets. Like all cycads, it is extremely slow-growing but exceptionally long-lived — specimens hundreds of years old are known. Cycas thouarsii is somewhat more cold-tolerant than other large Cycas species and performs well in zones 9–12, making it among the most useful large cycads for Gulf Coast, Florida, and warm subtropical gardens. A distinctive and dramatic specimen plant for large tropical-style landscape settings. All parts of the plant contain cycasin and other neurotoxins — not edible and should be kept away from children and pets.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Cycas thouarsii

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 8–11; Mexican Cycad is a beautiful, medium-sized understory cycad native to the cloud forests and moist mountain forests of eastern Mexico (Veracruz, Oaxaca, Chiapas, Tamaulipas) — one of the most ornamental and relatively shade-tolerant of the Ceratozamia cycads, with glossy, dark-green, arching fronds and good cold tolerance down to zone 8.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; Ceratozamia mexicana is more moisture-demanding than many cycads, reflecting its cloud forest origin — maintain consistent soil moisture with organic mulch and water regularly during dry periods. Ceratozamia mexicana (Mexican Cycad, Chamal) is an attractive, medium-sized cycad genus endemic to Mexico, with C. mexicana occurring primarily in the humid cloud forests, montane forests, and pine-oak forests of eastern Mexico — Veracruz, Tamaulipas, Oaxaca, and Chiapas — at elevations of 1,500–2,500 meters. Unlike many cycads adapted to dry, sunny conditions, Ceratozamia mexicana is a forest understory plant adapted to partial shade, consistent moisture, and cool, humid cloud-forest conditions — making it one of the more shade-tolerant cycads available for garden cultivation. The arching, compound fronds with broad, dark-green leaflets are highly ornamental, and the plants develop a distinctive trunk that remains partially buried or low-growing for many years before elongating. The genus name 'Ceratozamia' (horned zamia) refers to the distinctive paired horn-like projections on the pollen-cone scales. Listed as Threatened on the IUCN Red List due to forest clearance; never collect from the wild. All cycad parts contain neurotoxic cycasin glycosides.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 8–11; Prickly Cycad is one of the most widely cultivated and shade-tolerant of the Encephalartos cycads, native to the coastal forests and forest margins of eastern South Africa (KwaZulu-Natal, Eastern Cape) — producing large, dark-green, glossy fronds with spiny leaflets in a lush, tropical-looking crown, and growing well in considerably more shade than most cycads.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; Encephalartos villosus prefers more moisture and shade than most Encephalartos species — mulch the root zone and water moderately to maintain the moist, humus-rich forest-floor conditions of its native coastal forest habitat. Encephalartos villosus (Prickly Cycad or Villous Cycad) is one of the most horticulturally popular and widely cultivated Encephalartos species, native to the dense, subtropical coastal forests and forest margins of eastern South Africa from the Eastern Cape north through KwaZulu-Natal. Unlike many Encephalartos species adapted to open, dry, sunny habitats, E. villosus naturally inhabits shaded forest understory positions — making it one of the most shade-tolerant of all Encephalartos species and an excellent choice for partially shaded garden positions where many cycads would struggle. The fronds are large (up to 2 meters long), arching, dark-green, glossy, and set with numerous spiny leaflets that give the plant a bold, tropical texture. The common name 'Prickly Cycad' refers to these spiny leaflets. Plants can grow as acaulescent (nearly stemless) individuals for many years before developing a trunk. Encephalartos villosus is CITES Appendix II listed; only purchase nursery-propagated plants from reputable suppliers and never remove plants from the wild. All cycad parts are toxic.
sources: Cycad Society of South Africa — Encephalartos villosus

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 8–11; Cabbage Tree Palm is the only palm native to the Australian mainland east of the Great Dividing Range — a tall, stately, single-trunked fan palm from Queensland and New South Wales coastal rainforests and wet lowland forests, producing a large crown of deeply divided, glossy, green fan fronds and adapting well to cooler subtropical and warm-temperate climates.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Livistona australis tolerates periods of seasonal flooding and performs well in consistently moist soils — maintain soil moisture with mulch and water deeply; provide wind protection when young. Livistona australis (Cabbage Tree Palm or Australian Fan Palm) is a tall, elegant, single-trunked fan palm native to the wet coastal lowlands, rainforest margins, and swampy lowland forests of Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria — the only palm naturally occurring in southeastern Australia and one of the most cold-tolerant of all Livistona species. The trunk grows slowly and steadily over many years, eventually reaching 60–100 feet, topped by a large, rounded, dense crown of deeply divided, pendulous, glossy, bright-green circular fan fronds. The common name 'Cabbage Tree Palm' references the edible growing tip (the 'cabbage' or heart of palm) which was eaten by indigenous Australians and early settlers — though removing the growing tip kills the palm. Livistona australis is a popular landscape palm across Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, the Mediterranean, and warm subtropical regions of North America for its elegant form, tolerance of coastal conditions, and cold hardiness — one of the few large fan palms surviving brief freezing temperatures when established. Excellent specimen palm for large landscapes in zone 8b–11.
sources: Australian National Botanic Gardens — Livistona australis
Royal Botanic Gardens Sydney — Livistona australis

planting.note: Plant container stock or suckers in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Sago Palm is a large, clustering, monocarpic palm native to the peat swamps and lowland swamp forests of Southeast Asia — the primary source of sago, the starch extracted from the pith of the trunk immediately before flowering — a fundamental starch staple for millions of people in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Papua New Guinea.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in tropical conditions; Metroxylon sagu thrives in peat swamp conditions with consistently high water table — it tolerates seasonal flooding and requires consistently moist to wet soil; do not allow the root zone to dry out. Metroxylon sagu (Sago Palm) is a large, clustering, monocarpic palm native to the peat swamps, freshwater swamps, and lowland swamp forests of the Malay Archipelago — Borneo, Sumatra, the Malay Peninsula, the Moluccas, and Papua New Guinea — where it is the primary source of 'sago,' a powdery starch extracted from the pithy interior of the trunk immediately before the palm flowers and dies. Each trunk accumulates up to 150–300 kilograms of starch over 7–15 years before flowering, fruiting, and dying (monocarpic); the clusters produce multiple offshoots (suckers) that replace the mother stem, so the clump persists indefinitely. Sago is a fundamental caloric staple for millions of people across Indonesia, Malaysia, Papua New Guinea, and the Pacific Islands — prepared as papeda (sago porridge), sago pancakes, sago noodles, and diverse traditional foods. It has been called 'the staff of life of swamp peoples' and feeds communities in areas too wet, flooded, or acidic for rice or other crops. Globally important as a swamp-agroforestry tree for waterlogged, acidic, peat-swamp soils where no other major food crop survives.
sources: FAO Food and Agriculture Organization — Sago Palm (Metroxylon sagu)
PROSEA Palm Heart and Other Products — Metroxylon sagu

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral, low-fertility soil in zone 10–12; Silver Palm is an elegant, slow-growing Caribbean native fan palm from the Florida Keys, Bahamas, Cuba, and Hispaniola — adapted to alkaline, rocky, coastal limestone soils where few other palms thrive — bearing beautiful, circular, silver-backed fan fronds that shimmer in coastal breezes and small, round, dark-purple, edible berries.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, rocky to sandy, alkaline to neutral, lean soil — Silver Palm is highly adapted to coastal limestone conditions; avoid clay, overwatering, or organically enriched soil that promotes lush growth at the expense of cold and drought tolerance. Coccothrinax argentata (Silver Palm or Florida Silver Palm) is an elegant, slow-growing, single-trunked fan palm native to the rocky coastal pinelands, hammocks, and scrublands of the Florida Keys, the Bahamas, Cuba, Hispaniola, and other Caribbean islands, where it inhabits the harshest, most exposed coastal limestone environments. The palm is named for the distinctive silvery-white underside of the fan fronds — circular, deeply divided, dark-green above and shimmering silver-white beneath — which catch the light beautifully in coastal breezes, creating a distinctive two-tone shimmer. The small (1/2 inch), round, dark-purple to black berries that follow the small, creamy, fragrant flowers are edible and eaten by birds, and reportedly used in Caribbean folk medicine. Coccothrinax argentata is native to the calcareous, thin, nutrient-poor soils of the Caribbean limestone environment and is extremely well adapted to South Florida's alkaline soils — growing vigorously where many palms suffer from iron deficiency and limestone toxicity. An outstanding accent palm for South Florida and Caribbean gardens, especially in dry, exposed, coastal positions with alkaline soil.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Coccothrinax argentata
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Caribbean Palm Collection
